text: The patient is positioned with the right arm stretched out and then brought below body level. A Veress needle is introduced to seal the stoma site. Three ports are marked at a distance from the stoma site for the parastomal hernia repair procedure.
The robotic system is being docked for the hernia repair procedure, utilizing a three-arm technique while the fourth arm remains unused.
The video shows the peristomal hernia site, with the surgical team considering the use of an accessory trocar for assistance, although it is often not needed.

The surgeon begins the hernia repair using unipolar scissors on the right and bipolar forceps on the left, reducing the omentum from the parastomal hernia. Dissection lines are marked on the screen as the surgeon enters the retro-rectus space with monopolar scissors.
The surgeon develops the retrorectus space, progressing superiorly. Upon reaching the lateral edge, further dissection is performed.
The surgeon develops the tower plane, moving out from the linea seminalis to achieve a tension-free closure. The unilateral tower technique is being performed.

The neurovascular bundles are visible above the scissors as the surgeon operates in the correct TAR plane. The shining peritoneum confirms the plane's accuracy. The articulating left-sided forceps are used to open the plane, while the articulating scissors achieve a precise ninety-degree angle to facilitate efficient dissection.
The surgeon moves around the stoma, freeing the tissue planes and creating a buttonhole defect laterally, completing the dissection in this area.
The surgeon uses PDS StrataFix sutures to close the hernia defect, with the articulating robotic arms facilitating the closure process. The defect is visibly closed.
The surgeon obliterates the lateral space by suturing the lateral edge of the mesocolon to the adjacent muscle plane, ensuring secure attachment.

A tape is placed to measure the size of the dissection area, followed by cutting the mesh to fit. The buttonhole defect around the colon is closed from inferior to superior. The surgeon introduces a polypropylene mesh to lateralize the colon using a modified Sugarbaker technique, with retro-rectus placement.

The surgeon sutures the corners of the polypropylene mesh in place using robotic instruments. Following the mesh fixation, the posterior sheath and peritoneum are closed, marking the completion of the hernia repair surgery.
